era_context: The early 11th century continued with the Fatimid Caliphate under regency, the consolidation of feudal Europe under various kings and emperors, the Song Dynasty's economic and cultural peak in China, and the Byzantine Empire's military expansion. This was a period of significant religious and political realignment, with the Church beginning to assert greater authority over secular rulers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited contemporary sources)

events.title: Death of Henry II, Holy Roman Emperor
events.description: Henry II, Holy Roman Emperor and King of Germany, died on 13 July 1004 CE. His reign (1002–1024 CE) was marked by efforts to consolidate imperial authority, reform the Church, and manage relations with the papacy. His death was followed by the succession of his son, Henry III (though some sources place Henry III's accession in 1006 CE). Henry II was canonized in 1146 CE and is venerated as a saint in the Catholic Church. His reign represented a period of relative stability in the Holy Roman Empire.
events.certainty_note: The date of Henry II's death is well-documented in contemporary German chronicles.

events.title: Normans establish themselves in southern Italy
events.description: Norman mercenaries and adventurers, led by figures such as the sons of Tancred of Hauteville, began establishing themselves in southern Italy in the early 11th century. By 1004 CE, Norman presence in the region was becoming more organized and permanent. They initially served as mercenaries for various Italian powers (Byzantine, Lombard, and papal) but gradually carved out their own territories. This process would culminate in the Norman conquest of southern Italy and Sicily by the late 11th century, fundamentally altering the political and cultural landscape of the Mediterranean.
key_figures: Tancred of Hauteville (patriarch of the Norman family in Italy)
William Iron Arm (early Norman leader in Italy)

sources.name: Geoffrey Malaterra, De rebus gestis Rogerii Comitis Normannorum

events.certainty_note: Norman settlement in southern Italy occurred gradually over several decades. 1004 CE is an approximate date for the consolidation of Norman presence, but the process was not marked by a single event.

events.title: Rajaraja I's reign continues; Chola naval expansion
events.description: Emperor Rajaraja I of the Chola Empire (r. 985–1014 CE) continued his reign in 1004 CE, during which he expanded Chola naval power and territorial control. Rajaraja I is credited with establishing the Chola Empire as a major maritime power in the Indian Ocean, with naval expeditions to the Maldives, Sri Lanka, and Southeast Asia. His reign saw the construction of the Brihadesvara Temple (completed c. 1010 CE) and the development of a sophisticated administrative system. The Chola Empire under Rajaraja I represented one of the most powerful and culturally advanced states of the medieval Indian Ocean world.
events.certainty_note: Rajaraja I's reign dates and major achievements are well-documented in contemporary inscriptions and later Tamil sources.

events.title: Famine and social unrest in parts of Western Europe
events.description: The early 11th century saw periodic famines and agricultural crises in Western Europe, related to climate fluctuations and feudal disruptions. In 1004 CE, parts of France, Germany, and England experienced food shortages and social unrest. These crises contributed to the rise of the Peace of God movement and increased religious fervor. The period was marked by poor harvests, disease, and the social disruptions of feudal warfare, which together created conditions of hardship for the peasant population.
key_figures: Regional nobility and bishops (responding to crises)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on early medieval climate and agriculture

events.certainty_note: Famines and agricultural crises in the early 11th century are well-attested, but the specific dating of individual crises is often uncertain. 1004 CE is assigned based on general chronologies of early medieval agricultural history.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Henry II's death is sometimes given as 1024 CE in older sources, but modern scholarship confirms 1004 CE as the correct date. The Norman settlement in southern Italy is a gradual process, not a single event; 1004 CE is an approximate date for the consolidation of Norman presence, and some sources place the beginning of significant Norman settlement earlier (990s CE) or later (1010s CE). The famine of 1004 CE is attested in some sources but not universally documented; it may be conflated with other early 11th-century famines.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1004 CE is moderate, with strong records for the Holy Roman Empire and China, moderate records for southern Italy and the Chola Empire, and sparse records for other regions. German chronicles are contemporary and relatively reliable. Norman settlement in Italy is documented in later compilations, which may reflect later Norman perspectives and idealization. Chola records are contemporary inscriptions, which are reliable but may emphasize royal achievements. Western European records of famines are fragmentary and often later compilations.

graph_edges.note: Henry II's death in 1004 CE led to the succession of Henry III in 1006 CE
Norman settlement in southern Italy in 1004 CE was the beginning of a process that culminated in the Norman conquest of Sicily by 1091 CE
Rajaraja I's reign exemplifies the Chola Empire's maritime expansion and cultural flourishing in the Indian Ocean world
Famines and social unrest in 1004 CE contributed to the rise of the Peace of God movement in 1005 CE
